metadata.summary: The five Scrum events with time-boxes, facilitation patterns, quality gates, and Definition of Ready discipline.

content.title: Scrum Ceremonies
content.summary: The five Scrum events — Sprint Planning, Daily Standup, Sprint Review, Sprint Retrospective, and Backlog Refinement — with time-boxes, facilitation patterns (including async/remote variants), tool configurations, and quality gates. Sprint goal must be falsifiable in one sentence; retro actions must have an owner and a linked issue or they do not exist; async standup blockers must escalate within 1 working day.

why.p: Ceremony theatre — running events without the underlying DoR/DoD discipline and action follow-through — is the most common Scrum failure mode. The five events exist to create inspect-and-adapt loops at different cadences, not to generate reports for management. Each event has a specific output and a quality gate; agents can automate prep and capture, but cannot replace facilitation.

list.item: Bootstrapping Scrum for a new team and wiring ceremonies into a PM tool (Jira, Linear, GitHub Projects).
Replacing free-form standups with structured cadence after onboarding or merging teams.
Optimizing a remote/distributed Scrum team — async standups, retro tools, recorded reviews.
Reducing ceremony time-tax (pre-refine with an agent the day before planning).
Evidence collection for transformations (sprint goal achievement, retro action follow-through, velocity stability).
Solo developer or pair — Scrum overhead exceeds value; use Kanban with lightweight reviews.
Pure research or discovery teams with no incremental delivery — use Lean Startup or Discovery Kanban.
Crisis or incident periods — break glass, run incident process, restart Scrum after.
Hardware-heavy programs where 2-week sprints do not match material lead times.
Teams with working ceremonies and good outcomes — do not redesign for novelty.

section.name: time-boxes-per-sprint-length
section.p: Time-boxes scale with sprint duration. Always adhere to the time-box and end on time even if unfinished.

list.item: Sprint Planning: 2h (1-week sprint), 4h (2-week), 8h (4-week).
Daily Standup: 15 min (all sprint lengths).
Sprint Review: 1h (1-week), 2h (2-week), 4h (4-week).
Sprint Retrospective: 45min (1-week), 1.5h (2-week), 3h (4-week).
Backlog Refinement: 1-2h (1-week), 2-4h (2-week), 4-8h (4-week).

rule.statement: End every ceremony on time even if unfinished; recurring overruns indicate bad inputs (insufficient refinement, miscalculated capacity) — fix the input, not the time-box.
rule.rationale: Extending ceremonies trains the team that preparation is optional. The time-box creates the pressure that drives refinement discipline upstream.

section.name: sprint-planning

rule.statement: Never run sprint planning without a refined-Ready backlog of at least 1.5x sprint capacity; force a refinement session before planning if the backlog is not ready.
rule.rationale: Planning against unrefined stories produces a sprint goal that collapses on Day 2 when acceptance criteria are discovered to be missing.

section.p: Sprint Planning splits into two parts. Part 1 (What, first half): review sprint goal, top backlog items, acceptance criteria, and team questions. Part 2 (How, second half): break stories into tasks, estimate, identify dependencies, commit to sprint backlog.

rule.statement: Sprint goal must be falsifiable in one sentence; ban vague goals like "make progress on X". Reject during planning and ask the PO to reframe.
rule.rationale: An unfalsifiable sprint goal cannot be assessed at review. The team can always claim they "made progress" regardless of what was delivered.

section.reference: Sprint planning notes template for sprint info, goal, team capacity, committed backlog, stretch goals, risks/dependencies, and action items.

section.name: daily-standup
section.p: Three standup formats suited to different team conditions. Choose one and stick with it for the sprint; rotate formats quarterly to prevent staleness.

list.item: Classic three questions: What did I do yesterday? What will I do today? Any blockers?
Walk-the-board (preferred for flow): right-to-left column sweep, focus on stale items and blockers.
Async standup: post by agreed time, thread discussions, hard limit — blocker older than 1 working day escalates to sync.

rule.statement: Design standup for team-to-team flow visibility, not for reporting to a manager; standup output goes to the team channel, not a management dashboard.
rule.rationale: Standups designed for management reporting shift the power dynamic from self-organizing team to supervised status update, eroding agile culture and psychological safety.

section.reference: Geekbot async standup configuration with questions and schedule settings.

section.name: sprint-review

rule.statement: Sprint review requires real stakeholders with decision authority; without them it is engineering show-and-tell with no feedback loop.
rule.rationale: The review's purpose is to inspect the increment and adapt the backlog based on stakeholder feedback. Without stakeholders, the inspect-and-adapt loop is broken.

section.reference: Gate script checking completion ratio, demoable items, demo environment, invited stakeholders, and escaped bug count.

section.name: sprint-retrospective

rule.statement: Retro action items must have a named owner, a due date, and a linked issue — or they do not exist. Review previous sprint actions before adding new ones.
rule.rationale: Unowned, unlinked actions appear identically in every retrospective. The recurring-action antipattern destroys retro credibility and team trust in the process.

section.p: Retrospective formats rotate to prevent staleness. Supported formats include Start-Stop-Continue (default), 4Ls (Liked/Learned/Lacked/Longed For), Mad-Sad-Glad, and Sailboat (goal/wind/anchor/rocks). Each format surfaces different themes and emotions.
section.reference: Retrospective template with metrics, format options, action table with owner/due/issue, and team health check.

section.name: backlog-refinement-definition-of-ready
section.p: Refinement is the ceremony where the team and PO prepare stories for upcoming sprints. Stories must reach Definition of Ready (DoR) before they can enter sprint planning.

rule.statement: A story is Ready for sprint planning only when all five DoR criteria are met: acceptance criteria defined, dependencies identified, estimate provided, fits in one sprint, and is testable.
rule.rationale: Stories entering planning without DoR criteria cause mid-sprint discovery of missing information, breaking the sprint goal and forcing scope changes under pressure.

section.name: planning-poker-and-estimation
section.p: Planning poker is a structured estimation technique that uncovers diverse thinking and prevents anchoring bias.
Scale: 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ?, coffee. Tools: planningpoker.com, scrumpoker-online.org, or Jira-integrated poker modules.
Process: PO presents story → team asks questions → everyone votes secretly → reveal simultaneously → discuss outliers → re-vote if needed → consensus recorded.

rule.statement: Keep agent estimation suggestions masked until after the team's first vote; surfacing suggestions before voting anchors all estimates to the LLM's output (anchoring bias).
rule.rationale: Anchoring bias on an LLM baseline eliminates the independent estimation diversity that makes planning poker useful. The value of poker is the outlier discussion, not the median.

section.p: T-Shirt Sizing correlation: XS very small, few hours → 1 point. S small, about 1 day → 2-3 points. M medium, 2-3 days → 5 points. L large, about 1 week → 8 points. XL very large, needs splitting → 13+ points.

antipattern.description: Ceremony theatre — team runs events but DoR/DoD discipline and action follow-through are missing.
antipattern.reason: Ceremonies without underlying discipline produce the appearance of Scrum without the inspect-and-adapt value. Agents that only schedule and notify reinforce theatre by making it effortless.

antipattern.description: Standup as management reporting — standup updates go to a manager dashboard, not the team channel.
antipattern.reason: Reporting-oriented standups shift power from the self-organizing team to the manager, eroding psychological safety and turning the event into a performance rather than a coordination mechanism.

antipattern.description: Retros without action follow-through — same actions appear sprint after sprint with no resolution.
antipattern.reason: Repeated unresolved actions signal that the team does not believe retro actions will change anything. Trust in the retro collapses; teams stop contributing honestly.

antipattern.description: PO convergence in planning poker — PO names a number first and team votes to match it.
antipattern.reason: Authority-anchored poker produces uniform votes from the first round. No disagreement means no discussion, which means the only person whose estimate was real was the PO — poker served no purpose.

antipattern.description: Async blocker rot — a blocker is posted in standup, no one responds, it ages past 24 hours in a thread.
antipattern.reason: Unresolved async blockers compound daily. A 24-hour blocker in a 10-day sprint consumes 10% of capacity silently. Hard limit: blocker older than 1 working day must trigger a sync or escalation.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Hard rules for scaling Scrum ceremonies:

list.item: Time-box hard. End on time even if unfinished — recurring overruns indicate bad inputs (refinement quality, capacity miscalc).
Never run planning without a refined-Ready backlog ≥ 1.5× sprint capacity; force a refinement session if missing.
Walk the board (right-to-left) at standup; prioritize flow over individual status. Agents that surface stale columns help.
Sprint goal must be falsifiable in one sentence; ban vague goals like "make progress on X". Reject during planning.
Retro action items have owner + due-date + linked issue or they do not exist; agents enforce.
Rotate facilitators; standardize ceremony agendas in a runbook so rotation is cheap.
Async standups: maximum blocker age = 1 working day; auto-escalate.
Capture sprint review feedback in the backlog with provenance (who said what); never leave it in chat.
Velocity is for forecasting, not management — never use it as a performance metric per person.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A ceremony-orchestrator agent owns the sprint clock: schedules each event in the team calendar, posts pre-reads (refined backlog, capacity, prior-sprint metrics), drafts agendas, and posts post-event summaries with action items linked to issues.

A refinement-coach agent runs against the backlog mid-sprint: flags stories without acceptance criteria, oversized stories, missing estimates, hidden dependencies, then opens a refinement-ready list for the PO.
A retro-synthesizer clusters retro contributions, surfaces themes, and writes a 1-page summary with new action items + status of previous ones.
A standup-bot collects async updates, computes blocker age, and pages a Scrum Master after thresholds.
Humans facilitate live; agents do prep + capture, never replace conversation.

list.item: ceremony-orchestrator (haiku) — schedule + agenda + summary; touches calendar, PM tool, chat.
refinement-coach (sonnet) — backlog quality checks, story splitting suggestions, INVEST scoring.
estimation-helper (sonnet) — proposes story-point estimates from historical reference issues; never auto-applies.
retro-synthesizer (sonnet) — clusters retro inputs, drafts summary, links actions to backlog.
standup-bot (haiku) — async question prompts, thread-summary, blocker-age escalation.
velocity-watcher (haiku) — alerts on velocity outliers, commitment ratio drift, scope creep.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when wiring agents into Scrum ceremonies:

list.item: Bots that "summarize standups" to managers turn the ceremony into reporting; restrict standup-bot output to the team channel.
Estimation suggestions from LLMs anchor humans (anchoring bias) — keep them masked until after first vote, or do not use.
Auto-clustering retros loses context (sarcasm, sentiment) — always show original notes alongside themes.
Async standup tools generate vast logs; without rotation/archive, retrieval becomes the bottleneck. Page-summarize weekly.
Calendar agents schedule across time zones poorly when DST flips — always use IANA tz names, never UTC offsets.
Reading sprint metrics out-of-context: the same 30-point velocity is "good" or "bad" depending on capacity, holidays, dependencies. Force the agent to include context.
Auto-closing stale stories at sprint end is a foot-gun; let the team triage spillover, not a rule.
Permission scope: a standup-bot writing into the issue tracker can create circular notification storms; whitelist target fields.
Human-in-the-loop checkpoints (mandatory): sprint goal acceptance, story splitting, retro action commitment, sprint-end spillover decision.

template.purpose: Sprint planning notes: sprint info, goal, team capacity, committed backlog, stretch goals, risks/dependencies, action items.
Retrospective template: sprint metrics, what went well, what to improve, action items with owner/due, previous action review, team health check.
Geekbot async standup configuration with questions and schedule settings.
Gate script: checks completion ratio, demoable items, demo environment, invited stakeholders, escaped bug count.
